findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'Confirm before build' hedge with an explicit ADR-style decision record: state 'Option A is the selected design (decided <date> by <author>)' directly in the plan so an executor can proceed without seeking external confirmation.
findings.finding: The plan recommends Option A but then says 'Confirm before build given the reducer blast radius,' leaving the executor uncertain whether Option A IS the committed decision or still pending approval — this is a deferred decision that could block an executor waiting for confirmation.
findings.location: Design options section — 'Recommendation: Option A … Confirm before build given the reducer blast radius'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the last sentence of the Recommendation with: 'Option A is the selected design, decided [date] by [author]; no further confirmation required before build.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command or test description to AC1, e.g. 'a unit test that calls sign_opcert_manifest(...) and asserts the resulting SIGNATURE event data contains envelope, ticket_id, material_fingerprint, and merged_log_commit with the correct values.'
findings.finding: AC1 (producer stores an op-cert) has no associated proving command or test description — unlike AC2 which names 'a test asserting replay round-trips it,' AC1 names only the behavioral expectation with no step to verify it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'A producer stores an op-cert (DSSE envelope from opcert.sign_opcert) on a ticket...'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: 'Verified by a unit test that calls `signing.sign_opcert_manifest(...)`, reads back the emitted event, and asserts `event.data` contains `{"envelope": ..., "ticket_id": ..., "material_fingerprint": ..., "merged_log_commit": ...}` with correct types.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the rollout test in AC3: describe what 'older clone' behavior means in test terms (e.g., 'a test that replays a SIGNATURE event containing the new `envelope` field through an instance of `process_signature` that does NOT read `envelope`, asserts the event is accepted without error and no state key is lost').
findings.finding: AC3 names a 'rollout test' for the preserve-and-ignore behavior of older clones but does not describe what this test simulates, how it is structured, or what assertion it makes — leaving the executor to design the test from scratch with no specification.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: '…older clones preserve-and-ignore the new field/event (rollout test)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Lock the ACs to the chosen option: update AC1 and AC2 to name the concrete storage location (e.g. `attestations[kind]` for Option A) rather than 'via the chosen storage model' / 'ticket's reduced state', so the ACs enforce the decision.
findings.finding: The Design Options section nominates Option A as the recommendation and says 'pick one — the decision this ticket exists to make', but the Acceptance Criteria use option-neutral language ('via the chosen storage model', 'ticket's reduced state') that is equally compatible with Option B. An implementer reading ACs alone could implement Option B (new state['opcerts'] map) without contradicting any AC, sending contributors in two directions.
findings.location: Design options section vs. AC lines 1 and 2
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, replace 'via the chosen storage model' with 'by extending the SIGNATURE event data with optional `envelope` + bound fields, stored into `attestations[kind]`'. In AC2, replace 'ticket's reduced state' with 'into `attestations[kind]`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description of the read helper to the plan body (module path, signature, and which state field it reads from) so AC3 maps to a concrete described deliverable.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies a 'read helper' that 'returns (envelope, bound_fields) for a ticket's op-cert of a given kind, or None when absent', but no corresponding deliverable is described anywhere in the plan body — not in Context, not in either Design Option, not in the producer/reducer description.
findings.location: AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the body, e.g.: 'A `signing.get_opcert(ticket_state, kind) -> Optional[tuple[dict, dict]]` helper reads `attestations[kind]["envelope"]` and `attestations[kind]["bound"]`, returning `(envelope, bound_fields)` or `None`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan to the rollout note (AC4 and/or Context), describing how to recover if the deploy fails after snapshots have been written with the new field (e.g. snapshot re-generation procedure, feature-flag to disable envelope writes, or a confirmed 'additive-only, old code ignores unknown fields' safety argument for the snapshot format).
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges forward-compat (older clones preserve-and-ignore) but provides no rollback/back-out plan for the reducer + SNAPSHOT/compaction fold change. Once new SIGNATURE events with `envelope` fields are written to snapshots, rolling back the deploy requires handling compacted state that contains the new field — this irreversible write path is not addressed.
findings.location: Context (blast-radius discussion) and AC4 (rollout note)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC4) requiring that the Option A architectural decision — including Option B rejection rationale and the cross-clone preserve-and-ignore contract — is recorded in an ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or equivalent design doc before the ticket is closed.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision (extending the SIGNATURE event schema with an optional `envelope` field as the op-cert storage model, with specific cross-clone rollout sequencing) but does not specify capturing this in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc. AC4 only mandates a rollout note, not a durable record of the architectural choice itself.
findings.location: Design options section / AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4: 'Documented rollout note AND an ADR (or CLAUDE.md entry) capturing: Option A chosen over Option B, rationale, and the preserve-and-ignore cross-clone contract.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC specifying error handling for the producer: define the behavior when `opcert.sign_opcert` or the ticket-write fails (e.g., raise a typed exception, surface to caller, no partial write), and ensure failures are logged/instrumented.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a producer (`signing.sign_opcert_manifest`) that performs a signing operation and writes a SIGNATURE event, but neither the ACs nor the design text specify error handling: what happens when `opcert.sign_opcert` raises (e.g., key unavailable, crypto failure) or when the ticket write fails.
findings.location: Design options / Option A description / AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or implementation note specifying that the producer is idempotent (e.g., checks for an existing op-cert of the given kind before writing, or that the reducer's last-write-wins semantics are explicitly the defined contract and duplicates are acceptable).
findings.finding: The plan does not address producer idempotency: if `sign_opcert_manifest` is called twice for the same ticket/kind (e.g., during a retry or reconcile re-run), two SIGNATURE+envelope events are written. The reducer's behavior with duplicate op-cert envelopes for the same kind is unspecified.
findings.location: AC1 / Option A / reducer description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or implementation note requiring that producer failures and reducer envelope-preservation errors are logged at an appropriate level (e.g., error log with ticket_id + kind), so operators can detect and diagnose op-cert storage failures.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new instrumentation-worthy failure points (opcert signing, new reducer branch, compaction fold extension) but no AC or design note requires observability (logs, metrics, or alerts) for these paths.
findings.location: Context / AC section (all ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure to the rollout note: either a feature flag that disables `sign_opcert_manifest`, or a documented 'redeploy prior image — old code ignores the optional envelope field, no data cleanup needed' statement, with a test or runbook entry confirming it.
findings.finding: The plan specifies deploy ordering (reconcile hosts first) but provides no explicit rollback procedure: if the new envelope field or producer causes a regression after deployment, there is no stated mechanism to undo quickly (e.g. a feature flag disabling the producer, or a documented 'deploy old image — old code ignores the optional field' statement).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 rollout note; Design Options — Option A rationale
findings.suggested_fix: Since the envelope field is optional and old code ignores it (already noted), the cheapest rollback is redeploying the old producer image. Make this explicit: add a one-line rollback note to AC4 — 'To roll back: redeploy prior reconcile-host image; existing envelope fields in SIGNATURE events are ignored by the old reducer, no migration needed.' Optionally gate the producer behind a `OPCERT_PRODUCER_ENABLED` env flag.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define concurrency and idempotency semantics for `sign_opcert_manifest`: state whether the call is idempotent (e.g. content-addressed dedup on `material_fingerprint`), whether concurrent callers are serialised (e.g. via ticket-level lock or a compare-and-swap on the event store), and add a test covering duplicate/concurrent invocations.
findings.finding: The plan does not address concurrent invocations of `sign_opcert_manifest` for the same ticket+kind (e.g. two reconcile hosts racing) or idempotency under at-least-once delivery, leaving a gap in the shared-state lifecycle for the new `attestations[kind].envelope` field.
findings.location: Context — 'producer in signing.py'; Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (producer stores an op-cert); AC2 (reducer preserves envelope)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that ≥3 of the child tickets (4214/ee0b/8f1d) will call `sign_opcert_manifest` as their write path, OR explicitly justify the wrapper as a required seam (e.g. it owns the `append_event` call + envelope serialisation so all producers share the same persisted shape); if only ee0b calls it, consider inlining into ee0b and making the shape the spec.
findings.finding: `sign_opcert_manifest` is a new abstraction with only one confirmed producing call-site in the plan (ee0b). 4214 reads but does not call it; 8f1d's call count is unspecified. Rule-of-Three requires ≥3 call-sites before a shared producer wrapper is non-premature.
findings.location: Design options — Option A: 'A new `signing.sign_opcert_manifest(...)` producer writes such an event using `opcert.sign_opcert`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either name the additional consumers of the read helper beyond 4214 (e.g. ee0b idempotency check, 8f1d migration read-before-write) to meet Rule-of-Three, or downgrade AC3 to describe the required *data shape* in `state['attestations'][kind]` rather than mandating a standalone helper function.
findings.finding: The 'read helper' returning `(envelope, bound_fields)` (AC3) has only one named consumer (4214). A dedicated helper function at this call-count is below the Rule-of-Three threshold when callers could read `state['attestations'][kind]` inline.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'A read helper returns `(envelope, bound_fields)` for a ticket's op-cert of a given kind, or None when absent'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC3's rollout compatibility claim: under Option A, older clones DO process SIGNATURE events (it is in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES) and silently drop the new `envelope` field — specify the rollout test as verifying field-drop safety (envelope absent from old-clone reduced state), not event-level preserve-and-ignore.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'older clones preserve-and-ignore the new field/event' for Option A's rollout compatibility, but SIGNATURE is in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (_version.py:109) — older clones actively dispatch to process_signature, they do not skip/ignore the event. The actual compat guarantee is weaker: old clones call `data.get('envelope')` and receive None, silently dropping the field. This is different from a genuine preserve-and-ignore (which applies to unknown event *types*). The plan's rollout AC and rollout test should be restated to reflect field-level drop compatibility, not event-level ignore — otherwise the rollout test will be written against the wrong invariant.
findings.location: Design options / Option A description; AC3 'older clones preserve-and-ignore the new field/event (rollout test)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'A read helper returns (envelope, bound_fields) for a ticket's op-cert of a given kind, or None when absent; older clones processing the extended SIGNATURE event drop the unknown `envelope` field from their reduced state (field-drop compat, not event-ignore — tested by asserting envelope is absent after replaying with a pre-extension process_signature).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the on-disk serialization form for the DSSE `envelope` field in the SIGNATURE event data: either a `dsse.encode()`-produced JSON string (following the `author_sig` precedent in `_seam.py:301`) or a base64-dict matching the DSSE wire format — and pin which form `process_signature` expects to preserve and the read helper expects to decode.
findings.finding: The plan says the SIGNATURE event `data` gains an optional `envelope` (described as a 'DSSE dict'), but `dsse.Envelope` is a frozen dataclass with `bytes` fields (`payload`, `signatures[*].sig`) that are not directly JSON-serializable. `append_event` serializes event data as JSON, so an unspecified serialization will cause a runtime `TypeError`. The established precedent in `_seam.py:301` uses `dsse.encode(envelope.payload_type, envelope.payload, [...])` → a JSON string stored as a field. The plan does not specify whether `envelope` is stored as (a) a `dsse.encode()`-produced JSON string, or (b) a parsed dict with base64-string fields matching the DSSE wire format `{payloadType, payload, signatures:[{keyid, sig}]}`; these two choices differ for the corresponding read path in `process_signature` and the read helper.
findings.location: Design Options / Option A bullet; AC item 1 ('via the chosen storage model'); AC item 2 ('reducer preserves the op-cert envelope')
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the `author_sig` precedent: store `envelope` as the JSON string returned by `dsse.encode(env.payload_type, env.payload, [{'keyid': s.keyid, 'sig': s.sig} for s in env.signatures])`. The read helper then calls `dsse.decode(record['envelope'])` to reconstruct the `dsse.Envelope` object.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the read helper function and its home module in the plan (e.g. `signing.get_opcert(state, kind) -> tuple[dsse.Envelope, dict] | None` or a function in `attest/opcert.py`), so 4214/ee0b/8f1d have a concrete import target.
findings.finding: AC item 3 calls for 'a read helper returns `(envelope, bound_fields)` for a ticket's op-cert of a given kind, or None when absent', but neither names the function nor specifies the module it lives in. Tickets 4214 (`verify_opcert.py`), ee0b (trusted-server producer), and 8f1d (migration) are all listed as consumers. The existing `_record_for_kind` in `signing.py` returns an attestation record dict (not a `(envelope, bound_fields)` tuple). Without naming the helper, the child-ticket implementers have no stable import target.
findings.location: AC item 3; Success Criteria ('4214 can read a stored op-cert to verify it; ee0b/8f1d can produce op-certs onto tickets')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact wire representation of the `envelope` field in the SIGNATURE event `data` (e.g. a base64-encoded dict matching `dsse.encode()`/`dsse.decode()` — `{payload: b64str, payloadType: str, signatures: [{keyid, sig: b64str}]}`), and specify that the read helper reconstructs a `dsse.Envelope` object (via `dsse.decode(json.dumps(stored_dict))` or equivalent) before returning it to callers like `verify_required_environment`.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the `dsse.Envelope` object (a frozen dataclass with `payload: bytes` and `Signature.sig: bytes` fields) is serialized into the SIGNATURE event `data` dict, nor how the read helper reconstructs a `dsse.Envelope` from the stored form. `canonical_bytes` uses plain `json.dumps` with no bytes-capable `default=`, so storing an unprocessed `dsse.Envelope` or a dict with raw `bytes` values will raise a `TypeError` at commit time. The plan names 'DSSE dict' without defining whether it is base64-encoded (matching `dsse.encode()` output), a pre-decoded dict, or a JSON string — and the read helper AC says it returns an `envelope` without specifying whether that is a `dsse.Envelope` object (required by `verify_required_environment`) or the raw stored dict. Without this contract, Option A's mechanism is incomplete end-to-end.
findings.location: Design options / Option A description; AC line 1 ('DSSE envelope'); AC line 3 ('A read helper returns (envelope, bound_fields)')
findings.suggested_fix: In Option A's description and AC line 3, add: 'The `envelope` field is stored in the event `data` as a JSON-serializable dict with base64-encoded `payload` and `sig` fields (the form produced by `json.loads(dsse.encode(...))` — matching the DSSE JSON envelope spec). The read helper reconstructs a `dsse.Envelope` by deserializing this dict (e.g. via `dsse.decode(json.dumps(stored_dict))`), ensuring `verify_required_environment` receives the correct type.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the parent epic (6d0d) to add `reducer/` (specifically `reducer/_processors.py`) to its file_impact and acknowledge the op-cert storage/producer keystone as a decomposition item, so the reducer change is explicitly within the parent's authorized scope — or map it to an existing parent AC (e.g. AC1's 'produced/verified via the substrate' could be extended to name the reducer preservation step).
findings.finding: The leaf's primary deliverable — changes to `reducer/_processors.py::process_signature` and the SNAPSHOT/compaction fold — is not present in the parent epic's declared scope, file_impact, ACs, or intended decomposition. The parent's file_impact names only `src/rebar/attest/`, `src/rebar/signing.py`, `.github/workflows/`, and `infra/`; `reducer/` appears nowhere. The leaf's own context confirms the parent 'treated this as implicit,' meaning the parent's plan genuinely does not contain this deliverable.
findings.location: Parent epic 6d0d-7580-e18f-48c3 — file_impact list and Intended decomposition; Leaf AC2 ('The reducer preserves the op-cert envelope + bound fields into the ticket's reduced state (and through the SNAPSHOT/compaction fold)')
findings.suggested_fix: In the parent epic's file_impact, add an entry: `{'path': 'src/rebar/reducer/', 'reason': 'extend process_signature and the SNAPSHOT/compaction fold to preserve the DSSE op-cert envelope + bound fields in attestations[kind]'}`. In the Intended decomposition, add a line: '0. Op-cert storage + producer primitive (keystone): reducer preservation of DSSE envelope + bound fields; signing.py producer; read helper — shared prerequisite for children 4214/ee0b/8f1d.' This brings the leaf fully within the parent's declared scope and re-grounds the parent's attestation to cover this work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact serialization contract for the DSSE envelope in SIGNATURE event data: confirm whether it is stored as (a) a `dsse.encode()`-produced JSON string (matching the `author_sig` pattern), (b) a `json.loads(dsse.encode(...))` plain dict, or another form; document how the reducer and read helper reconstruct an `Envelope` from it, and confirm the chosen form survives JSON round-trip through the event store and compaction snapshot.
findings.finding: The plan asserts an 'optional `envelope` (DSSE dict)' will be added to SIGNATURE event data and round-tripped through the reducer, but does not resolve the serialization format. The existing codebase stores DSSE envelopes as JSON strings via `dsse.encode()` (see `_seam.py:301` — `author_sig`), not as raw dicts. `dsse.Envelope` is a frozen dataclass with `bytes`-typed `payload`/`sig` fields that are not directly JSON-serializable. There is no `to_dict()` method and no established precedent in this codebase for storing a DSSE envelope as a dict in event data. Without specifying this contract, the producer (signing.py), the reducer (`process_signature`), the compaction fold, and the AC3 read helper cannot be implemented consistently.
findings.location: Design Options / Option A description; AC1 ('optional `envelope` (DSSE dict)'); AC2 ('reducer preserves the op-cert envelope'); AC3 ('read helper returns (envelope, bound_fields)')
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the established `author_sig` pattern: store the envelope as `dsse.encode(envelope.payload_type, envelope.payload, [...])` (a JSON string) in the SIGNATURE event data field, and have the read helper call `dsse.decode(record['envelope'])` to reconstruct the `Envelope`. This is the only path with a proven JSON round-trip in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback note to AC4 that asserts the post-rollback schema state: specify that rolling back the reducer change leaves tickets whose SNAPSHOTs embed `envelope` in compiled_state projecting `None` for their op-cert (a known acceptable regression, or mitigated by a config flag / re-compact step), and confirm whether this constitutes a DATA LOSS RISK or an acceptable transient gap.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema STATE of reduced ticket state after a rollback of the reducer change: SNAPSHOTs written post-upgrade embed 'envelope' in compiled_state; a rolled-back (old) compactor calling process_snapshot restores compiled_state verbatim but subsequent SIGNATURE event replays via old process_signature silently drop 'envelope' — the op-cert disappears from reduced state without any error, causing 4214 (verifier) to see None for op-certs on already-stored tickets. The plan says 'older clones preserve-and-ignore' but this describes new events read by old code, not the projection state after rollback.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('Documented rollout note') and Design Options — Option A rationale ('older clones preserve-and-ignore the new field/event')
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the attestations-rollout pattern from docs/migrations.md: add a config flag (e.g. `compact.include_opcert_envelope = false`) that lets operators suppress writing `envelope` into new SNAPSHOTs compiled_state before rolling back the reducer, so any compaction that runs after the flag is set produces SNAPSHOTs that are safe for old process_signature to replay. Document the post-rollback state explicitly: 'After rollback, tickets last compacted post-upgrade will have no envelope in reduced state until re-compacted; verify-opcert falls back to advisory (no enforced failure on absent op-cert).' Add this to AC4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `sign_opcert_manifest`'s full parameter list, return type, module location (`signing.py`?), and internal protocol (what SIGNATURE event fields it populates) so child tickets 4214/ee0b/8f1d have a concrete interface to code against.
findings.finding: `sign_opcert_manifest(...)` is named in the plan as the new producer but its parameter list, return type, module location, and internal protocol are never defined, and the function does not exist anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Design options — Option A description: 'A new `signing.sign_opcert_manifest(...)` producer writes such an event using `opcert.sign_opcert`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: `signing.sign_opcert_manifest(ticket_id: str, material_fingerprint: str, merged_log_commit: str, *, key_path: str, principal: str, repo_root=None) -> None` — calls `opcert.sign_opcert(...)`, serializes the returned `Envelope` via `dsse.encode(...)`, and appends a SIGNATURE event with the envelope JSON string (or dict) in `data['envelope']` plus `data['ticket_id']`, `data['material_fingerprint']`, `data['merged_log_commit']`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact wire format for `data['envelope']` in the SIGNATURE event (e.g., 'the JSON string produced by `dsse.encode(payload_type, payload, signatures)`') and the corresponding deserialization step in the read helper (e.g., `dsse.decode(record['envelope'])`) so producer, reducer, and reader agree on the round-trip format.
findings.finding: The serialization format for storing a `dsse.Envelope` (a frozen dataclass with `payload: bytes` and `Signature.sig: bytes`) into the SIGNATURE event's JSON `data` dict is never specified, yet both the producer and `process_signature`'s preserve logic must agree on this format.
findings.location: Design options — Option A: 'Add an optional `envelope` (DSSE dict)' / AC2: 'The reducer preserves the op-cert envelope + bound fields'
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt `dsse.encode()` as the canonical form: producer sets `data['envelope'] = dsse.encode(envelope.payload_type, envelope.payload, [{'keyid': s.keyid, 'sig': s.sig} for s in envelope.signatures])`; read helper calls `dsse.decode(record['envelope'])` to recover `dsse.Envelope`. This matches the existing identity command's pattern at `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py:413`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the read helper's module, name, full parameter list, and return type (e.g., `def get_opcert(state: dict, kind: str) -> tuple[dsse.Envelope, dict] | None` in `attest/opcert.py` or `reducer/_processors.py`) so children 4214/ee0b can import and use it.
findings.finding: The 'read helper' mandated by AC3 is referenced but entirely unspecified: its module location, function name, parameter list, and return type (in particular whether `envelope` is a `dsse.Envelope` dataclass or a raw dict) are never defined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'A read helper returns `(envelope, bound_fields)` for a ticket's op-cert of a given kind, or None when absent'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
